cataloging_subject.label: Life of Soul (version L)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English prose work Life of Soul as represented by version L in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Laud Misc. 210, fols. 114r-132v. The cataloged intellectual unit comprises six question-and-answer exchanges, the last of which occupies most of the tract. It is not the Laud codex as a physical object, versions A or H as interchangeable copies, Moon's three-witness edition, Schaffner's modern translation or abridgment, the U-M encodings, or the checked-in repository XML. Versions A and H remain collateral textual versions in the lineage record rather than content silently merged into version L.

catalog_summary.title: Life of Soul (version L)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.editor: Paul F. Schaffner (transcription, editing, and electronic markup)
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth century or about 1400; exact composition date unresolved
catalog_summary.region: English Midlands; exact place of composition unresolved

notes: The version qualifier exposes the exact Laud-based boundary. It does not claim that L is a separate medieval work unrelated to A and H.
The spelling appears as the XML header's TYPE=130 title; it is not represented as an authorial rubric in version L.

titles.value: Frend in crist as seynt poule seiþ· We ne hauen here no cyte· þat is dwelling· but we sechen on þat is to come here after·

scope.description: Opening of version L after the editorial [question] speaker label

titles.value: Sire in þes two comaundementes of loue is al goodnesse fulfild· & al euel eschewed· & so þe lyuynge of oure soule & þe riȝt weye to heuene is to be leuyn stedefastliche in crist & to kepe þes two comaundementes of loue.

notes: The physical manuscript named in the inherited XML AUTHOR field is not an intellectual author.
The dialogue speakers are anonymous roles and are not evidence for an author name.

scope.description: Version-L transcription, editorial intervention, notes, and electronic presentation

notes: This is a transmission-layer responsibility, not authorship of the medieval work.

scope.description: Near-diplomatic transcription of version L from an unidentified film of Laud Misc. 210, fols. 114r-132v

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth century or about 1400; exact date unresolved

assertion.notes: Schaffner says about 1400 or a little earlier, Macmillan calls the work late fourteenth century, and MED uses a c1400 citation stencil. These formulations are compatible but do not justify a narrow normalized range.

notes: The fifteenth-century dates of the three surviving codices are witness dates, not composition dates.

date_statements.display: First half of the fifteenth century in Huntington Library HM 502; the relative copying order of L, A, and H remains unresolved

scope.description: Earliest narrowly dated surviving witness currently verified, not necessarily the earliest copy

assertion.notes: The Huntington catalog dates HM 502 between 1400 and 1450. Laud Misc. 210 and Arundel 286 are currently cataloged only to the fifteenth century.

places.label: English Midlands
notes: A broad linguistic and cultural attribution from Schaffner's headnote; no precise place of composition is asserted.
This does not assign Oxford, London, San Marino, or Ann Arbor as the place of medieval composition; those are holding or digital-production places.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Biblical references in Schaffner's notes are editorial apparatus; they are not a secondary language of the medieval work.

form_statements.description: Continuous Middle English prose organized as six alternating questions and answers; the final answer forms most of the tract.

subject_statements.label: Commandments, deadly sins, virtues, and works of mercy
Biblical quotation and exposition
notes: Schaffner estimates that more than half of the tract consists of biblical quotation; this record does not convert that estimate into a computed percentage.

assertion.notes: The intended audience is not securely recoverable; Schaffner argues that version L appears suited to a lay audience while acknowledging that its counsels also fit religious readers.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English catechetical and devotional prose dialogue on how the soul reaches heaven. Five short preliminary exchanges establish Christ as the life of the soul and ask what nourishes that life; the long sixth answer develops faith in Christ and obedience to the two commandments of love through the creed, Ten Commandments, seven deadly sins, corporal works of mercy, and six virtues. Biblical quotation and allusion carry much of the argument, while repeated emphasis on motive presents good works as the fruit of love rather than an external checklist.

Life of Soul survives in three versions, each currently known from one manuscript: L in Bodleian MS. Laud Misc. 210, fols. 114r-132v; A in British Library Arundel MS 286, fols. 115r-129r; and H in Huntington HM 502, fols. 35r-60v. The repository text is Schaffner's version-L transcription, ultimately made from an unidentified film of Laud, not Moon's edition and not a collation of all three witnesses.

Schaffner identifies features compatible with Wycliffite sympathy, including treatment of oaths, Eucharistic language, dominion, lay piety, and vernacular scripture, but explicitly calls the evidence inconclusive. The record therefore retains a qualified possible-Wycliffite-sympathies research tag and an open question rather than assigning the work or author categorically to Lollardy.

extent.description: One unheaded DIV1 contains 12 alternating SP and SPEAKER elements: six [question] and six [answer] turns. The body has 62 P elements and 38 continuous folio milestones from 114r through 132v. It also has 121 NOTE1, 49 ADD, 30 DEL, 29 SUPPLIED, 17 UNCLEAR, 7 ITEM, 4 SIC, 2 CORR, and 2 GAP elements. These are encoding metrics; the six exchanges are not separately headed divisions, and the final answer contains most of the tract.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / pinned CME source XML, lifesoul.xml.
evidence.summary: The 81,942-byte file identifies U-M/DLPS item lifesoul, Paul F. Schaffner as editor, Laud Misc. 210 fols. 114r-132v as version-L source, a 2003 electronic publication, and the complete six-exchange prose transcription. It also embeds a restrictive 1999 availability notice and incorrectly places the physical manuscript in AUTHOR.

evidence.citation: Reproducible XPath and checksum inspection of the pinned lifesoul.xml.
evidence.summary: Counts 1 DIV1, 12 SP, 12 SPEAKER, 62 body P, 38 folio MILESTONE, 121 NOTE1, 49 ADD, 30 DEL, 29 SUPPLIED, 17 UNCLEAR, 7 ITEM, 4 SIC, 2 CORR, and 2 GAP elements. Speaker labels alternate six [question] and six [answer] turns. XPath serialization of TEXT has SHA-256 a7465206c43eac56d296fef026019fdc09e5192473a4d9b628f8b6473ee538c2 in both the pinned file and Schaffner's live 2016 XML.

evidence.citation: Paul F. Schaffner, Life of Soul electronic-edition index and revision history.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the complete and abridged deliverables, 1997-2003 revision history, 2016 XML layer, and separate rights terms for transcriptions versus translation, headnote, and notes.

evidence.citation: Paul F. Schaffner, complete HTML transcription of MS Laud Misc. 210.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the direct HTML transcription, its opening and ending, folio span, markup conventions, revision date, and current public-domain dedication.

evidence.citation: Paul F. Schaffner, Life of Soul (version L), 1999 TEI P3 SGML encoding.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the title, anonymous c1400 authorship statement, Schaffner responsibility, direct Laud source, and conversion of the HTML transcription into TEI P3 SGML.

evidence.citation: Paul F. Schaffner, 2003 readable TEI P3 SGML encoding of Life of Soul.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for Schaffner's transcription, editing, and markup responsibility and for the 2003 removal of most line breaks and EXPAN markup, addition of translation-based paragraphs and biblical notes, and folio-break adjustment.

evidence.citation: Paul F. Schaffner, editorial headnote to Life of Soul.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for work date and region, six-exchange structure, catechetical and devotional contents, three-version witness census, Moon/Pimpl edition history, Laud microfilm source, audience discussion, and explicitly inconclusive Wycliffite-sympathy argument.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Laud Misc. 210.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the official shelfmark, ARKs, composite two-part parchment codex, fifteenth-century English production, contents-level limits, and catalog revision history.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Arundel MS 286.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for version A at fols. 115r-129r, its rubric and incipit, fifteenth-century vellum codex, physical access, and absence of linked digitised content.

evidence.citation: The Huntington Library, Devotional miscellany, mssHM 502.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for version H at fols. 35r-60v, its incipit and explicit, 1400-1450 codex date, restricted physical access, selected digital images, and Moon's Laud-based collation against Arundel and HM 502.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary bibliography BIB2110 and citation stencil Life Soul (LdMisc 210).
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage repertory evidence for the stable bibliography identifier and c1400 Life Soul (LdMisc 210) citation form.

evidence.citation: Sarah Macmillan, 'Well saved in suffering': male piety in late-medieval tribulation texts, Reading Medieval Studies 43 (2017), 55-78.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage scholarly evidence calling Life of Soul a late-fourteenth-century treatise and discussing it in the devotional context of Arundel 286.

evidence.citation: Sarah Macmillan, 'Suffering for Love: Compilation and Asceticism in Life of Soul' (2020).
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage metadata for the peer-reviewed chapter devoted specifically to Life of Soul in Late Medieval Devotional Compilations in England.

open_questions.question: Can current linguistic and textual scholarship narrow the composition date and regional localization beyond late fourteenth century or c1400 in the English Midlands?
next_steps: Consult Moon's full 1963/1978 linguistic commentary and Macmillan's 2020 chapter.
Check whether a current Linguistic Atlas or dialect study distinguishes authorial language from the three scribal versions.
Do not substitute witness production places or dates for composition evidence.

open_questions.question: Does current scholarship accept, qualify, or reject Schaffner's suggestion that Life of Soul may reflect Wycliffite sympathies?
next_steps: Review Macmillan's 2020 work-specific chapter and later scholarship for direct engagement with oath-taking, Eucharistic language, dominion, and vernacular biblical use.
Keep the present tag qualified and do not classify the anonymous author as a Lollard without stronger evidence.

notes: The XML's inherited AUTHOR value names Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 210 and is treated as a physical-source field mistakenly mapped into authorship.

content_structure_status is summary because the six exchanges are structurally recoverable but lack stable internal titles or separately encoded divisions; inventing six content parts would overstate the XML's hierarchy.

Schaffner's headnote supplies a three-version census, but its prose labels for the A/H interlocutor pairs conflict with the primary incipits and inspected HM 502 image; the lineage record preserves this as an unresolved descriptive conflict rather than propagating the role mapping.
